context.description: Chapter 4, "When Consciousness Makes Jumps," opens a section on dreams and other delusions. The author distances the argument from esoteric claims and presents dreams as a way to examine what consciousness becomes in sleep. The prose argues that dream experience is irrational, rule-free, and often forgettable from waking awareness.
normalization_notes: Translated German headings and prose into English control-language summary.
No exact book work title beyond the visible file-derived title.

rows.paragraph_text: The author rejects esoteric explanations and says consciousness can unfold beyond ordinary waking experience.
rows.text_content: The author rejects esoteric explanations and says consciousness can unfold beyond ordinary waking experience.

rows.paragraph_text: Dreams are presented as a subject that reveals what consciousness becomes in sleep and how waking awareness forgets what falls outside its light.
rows.text_content: Dreams are presented as a subject that reveals what consciousness becomes in sleep and how waking awareness forgets what falls outside its light.

rows.paragraph_text: Dreams are described as fantastical, rule-free worlds in which physics, causality, and continuity break down and thought becomes irrational.
rows.text_content: Dreams are described as fantastical, rule-free worlds in which physics, causality, and continuity break down and thought becomes irrational.
